---
summary: "How an app's iframe calls handlers through the window.centraid bridge, subscribes to the change stream, and receives theme + knob settings."
title: 'UI and changes'
---

# UI and changes

An app's `index.html` loads inside an iframe at `/centraid/<id>/`. The runtime **bakes a `window.centraid` bridge into every served HTML page**, so the page has three things wired for it: calling handlers, subscribing to the change stream, and receiving theme + knob settings from the shell.

## Calling handlers: `window.centraid`

Don't hand-roll `fetch` to the dispatcher — use the injected bridge. It already knows the app id (from the URL) and routes to the right tool endpoint (including the draft-preview route when you're previewing in the builder).

```js
// Read (centraid_read) — returns the handler's value directly.
const today = await window.centraid.read({ query: 'get-today', input: {} });

// Write (centraid_write) — returns the action's payload.
await window.centraid.write({ action: 'set-cups', input: { cups: 5 } });

// Describe (centraid_describe) — manifest + schema for this app.
const meta = await window.centraid.describe();
```

- `read({ query, input })` requires `query`; `write({ action, input })` requires `action`. Both reject if you omit the name.
- On failure the promise rejects with an `Error` whose `.code` (e.g. `INVALID_INPUT`, `UNKNOWN_QUERY`, `HANDLER_ERROR`) and `.status` are set from the dispatcher's error envelope. See [Reference → error codes](/reference/error-codes).

Under the hood the bridge POSTs to `/centraid/_tool/<name>` with `{ app, query|action, input }`. You can call that endpoint directly if you must, but the bridge handles the app id, the draft route, and error shaping for you.

## Subscribing to the change stream {#the-change-stream}

Every successful write — from an app action, the chat assistant, or the cloud SQL panel — emits a server-sent `change` event for the app. The bridge subscribes for you and fans events out through `window.centraid.onChange`:

```js
// Returns an unsubscribe function. Call once at startup, after refresh() exists.
const off = window.centraid.onChange((detail) => {
  // detail.tables : string[] — the tables actually mutated (precise, never ["*"])
  // detail.ts     : number   — commit time, ms since epoch
  // detail.source : "handler" | "agent" | "external"
  // detail.toolCallId? : string — present only when source === "agent"
  // detail.turnId?     : string — present only when source === "agent"
  void refresh();
});
```

The same payload is also dispatched as a DOM event, if you prefer:

```js
window.addEventListener('centraid:datachange', (e) => {
  // e.detail has the same { tables, ts, source, toolCallId?, turnId? } shape
  void refresh();
});
```

What the `tables` list contains is **observed from the SQL the write actually ran** — the runtime wraps writes in a SQLite session and decodes the touched tables. It is not read from the manifest's `writes[]` (see [Actions → the change-stream contract](/build/actions#the-change-stream-contract)). Empty changesets are suppressed, so you never get a no-op event.

`source` tells you who wrote:

- `"handler"` — an app action under `actions/`.
- `"agent"` — the chat assistant (a declared action via `centraid_write`, or the `_sql` built-in). Carries a `turnId` for the whole chat turn and a per-tool-call `toolCallId`.
- `"external"` — the cloud-style SQL panel or any other direct path.

The underlying transport is an `EventSource` on `/centraid/<id>/_changes` that auto-reconnects on transient drops; the bridge re-establishes it if the connection closes. You don't write reconnect logic.

### Filter by table

Skip the refetch when none of the changed tables back the part of the page you render:

```js
const dependsOn = {
  counter: ['hydrate_daily'],
  recap: ['hydrate_weekly_recaps'],
};

window.centraid.onChange((detail) => {
  const changed = new Set(detail.tables);
  for (const [section, deps] of Object.entries(dependsOn)) {
    if (deps.some((t) => changed.has(t))) refresh(section);
  }
});
```

Subscribe **once** at startup and read from derived state, rather than opening a separate stream per component.

## Receiving settings {#receiving-settings}

Theme and knob values reach the iframe two ways. **Initial values are server-baked**: the runtime stamps `<html data-<key>="…" style="--<key>:…">` onto the page before serving it, so the first paint is correct with no JavaScript. **Live changes** (the user flipping a knob or theme while the iframe is mounted) arrive via `postMessage`:

```js
window.addEventListener('message', (e) => {
  const d = e.data;
  if (!d) return;
  if (d.type === 'centraid:settings') applySettings(d);
  else if (d.type === 'centraid:theme') applyTheme(d.theme, d.bgL);
});

function applyTheme(theme, bgL) {
  const h = document.documentElement;
  if (theme === 'dark' || theme === 'light') h.dataset.theme = theme;
  if (bgL != null && bgL !== '') h.style.setProperty('--bg-l', bgL + '%');
}

function applySettings({ dataAttrs, cssVars }) {
  const h = document.documentElement;
  if (dataAttrs) for (const k in dataAttrs) h.setAttribute('data-' + k, dataAttrs[k]);
  if (cssVars) for (const k in cssVars) h.style.setProperty('--' + k, cssVars[k]);
}
```

- `centraid:settings` carries `dataAttrs` (→ `<html data-<key>>`) and `cssVars` (→ inline `--<key>`).
- `centraid:theme` carries `theme` (`"dark"` / `"light"`) and `bgL` (a background-lightness percentage).

Your CSS then reads from those data attributes / vars — see the four standard knobs (`appFont`, `appWidth`, `appRadius`, `appColor`) wired up in any scaffolded `app.css`. The scaffolded `index.html` ships this exact bridge inline (plus a URL-hash fallback for older preview paths); copy that pattern.

## CSP and inline scripts

Every HTML response is served with a strict `Content-Security-Policy` (`default-src 'self'`), so your app loads scripts, styles, fonts, and images only from `/centraid/<id>/`. **Put your logic in `.js` files** loaded with `<script type="module" src="app.js">` — that is the supported, same-origin path.

For the few inline `<script>` tags an app legitimately ships (e.g. the synchronous settings bridge in `index.html`), the runtime mints a fresh per-response CSP nonce and stamps it onto every inline script automatically — you do not add a `nonce` attribute yourself, and you cannot whitelist your own inline scripts beyond what the runtime stamps. Prefer external `.js` for everything else.

## Knobs flow, end to end

When a user changes a knob in the desktop's per-app settings popover:

1. The desktop persists the new value.
2. It posts `centraid:settings` to the iframe with the derived `dataAttrs` / `cssVars`.
3. Your `applySettings` handler updates the DOM.
4. Your CSS reads from the updated attributes / vars.

The knob is the _user-visible_ concept; what arrives in the iframe is the rendered set of attributes and CSS vars. Declare the knob in [`app.json → knobs`](/build/app-json#knobs); style it in `app.css`.
